speaker: Mamie 'Peanut' Johnson Goodman
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: I'm sitting here with Mamie Johnson Goodman. Hi, Ms. Goodman. Hi, darling. How are you? OK. I want to ask you something. First, which team did you play for? Well, I played for the NFL's count and overall, and then my pro-watt rate for St. Dickinson, the Alexander O'er the stars. OK. How many females played on the team? There were three. Down the stone. She was first. I was second. And then there was Connie more than there first. Who formed the team? There was a name by the name of Mr. Roon Foster in 1920. And that's where it was formed. I mean, that's the way it was formed. But where I did not know. OK. How old were you when you started playing? I was like very, very, very young. When I started playing balls down south. And that was all that we really had to do at that particular time. And I learned to play with the show. And there was a big news in Java. We had to make an on-based ball stand. And yes, the image on-based ball stand. And it was an enjoyable thing because that was our past time. And at that particular time, there was nothing else to do. So whatever the fellas did I do to never face them all. OK. How many do you remember my name plays on the team? Our team kept on scuse me 22. And when we were playing, we were playing to play as well as two. How did you feel being a female playing ball those days? I felt enjoyable. It was wonderful to me to know that I was good enough to be out there with the fellas. It made me proud because it was something that I had always wanted to do. And with that, it was wonderful to know, you know, just being there was just fine with me. What was it like playing for a Negro League? Well, see, what happened with that? And thank God for it. I tried to throw the fight, couragefully. And it looked at me like I was crazy as if to say what do you want. So, and thus God, the fellas came along about a year later. And I was like, I got to try out from a man by the name of Mr. Bish Tyson. He introduced me to Mr. Bernie Downs, who was the manager for the clowns. And I got to try out and I made it. How do you treat it being a black and beautiful, honey? I don't know. See, I haven't said this. Back then, the fellas were gentlemen. And I think there was a different green of young men back then than me. You know, we were raised different. And that made our character different. You know, it's a whole not different now than it was then. Young men came from families where they were taught character and respect and all those kind of things. And then there was an enjoyable thing for me to be there. So, there were white women, they were like, oh, yeah, you mean black? No, no. There's the white woman's league, well, it was, you know, highly recognized. But there were no black women. And I'm there. It was the segregation. Then just like it was for the fellas, it was for me to try to play with them. And then they just rejected me. I guess because I was black or whatever. But I felt like I was just as good, look, this is good and complete, just as good. So, it just thing Godfather fellas coming along and it was a tremendous thing for me. Okay. Did you have to trial for the team? Yes, yes, I tried out. And I went back and Mr. Dowsett told me to meet the boss. And I did, that I was gone. Did you share the same locker rooms or did they have different rooms? Well, what we did was we would either change before the guys or either after the guys. But we shared the same facilities. And a whole lot of times, there weren't any facilities for change. And we had to change on the bus. And in some places where we weren't even able to buy food, but we made up some kind of way, you know. So, that's where you go back to the segregation, but we knew it was there. So, it really didn't bother us because we knew it was there. And we knew that we weren't wanted. So, why put yourself in the position where you're not wanted? So, you know, it didn't bother us at all. What was your bad habits? Well, I think I got an average of about. Let me know here. Then so long ago, what did they say here? I have to go to. Two six and two two two eight and four. Yeah, that's what it is. What state have you played in? Every state there is. Canada and wherever. But I don't think there is a state that I haven't played yet. The team ever got out of the country? Yeah, we went to Canada, but some of the fellas doing the win or months went to Puerto Rico and whatever you played, but I never went. What were living conditions like? Well, we slept on the bus most of the time because we traveled like in the day because we played mostly night games. And when we did get to the nice towns, where they had nice hotels, we stayed in them. But, you know, we still segregated things. But the women would stay together. Oh, yes. The women would stay in the hotels or whatever. And then there were provisions made for the women. We would stay like in people's homes. And then there were very nice people. And the fellas would stay most of them to do the hotels or whatever. But we stayed in people's homes. We were very nice. Now, you need to actually, what type of work did you do before you started playing ball after you were playing ball? Okay. After I was playing ball, I came back and I went to nursing school and I retired as a nurse. But the ones that time you were playing ball, you didn't work with your friends? I didn't work. You worked in quite a round doing a great depression, I don't think. But do you remember any stories about that time for us baseball? You know, people want to say that. It was hard. But when you're doing something that you want to do and that you enjoy doing, it's not that hard because the hard part of it you don't even think about. You think about what you're doing. And then a lot of fellas want to say it was hard. You know, I didn't even put the older fellas that were there way before me. They don't emphasize the hardness of it because it's something they wanted to do. They were ball players and there wasn't much pain or whatever. But they enjoyed it, but something they wanted. If they did get pain. Yes, as far as I know they did. But they're a pinching that you received after the retire from baseball? No, no, no. We don't receive a pension that I know but some fellas do. I think that I was told but now it's just for that to be correct, I don't know. I know I don't. Doing the civil rights movement. Do you know if that effect is a team in any way? No. Because like I said before, we knew segregation was there. And we didn't put ourselves in the position for that to affect us because if I know that you don't want me at your house or whatever, then I'm not going to come there. And it's the same thing, but the restaurants or whatever. If I know that they don't want me then, then I'm going to say it be why am I going? I mean that's stupid. That's what happened with that. Do you have any idea where the other team players came from? Well, they came from every state there is to come from dollars. And there are books that you can find that will tell you where some of the players came from and whatever. And I don't think there's a state that's listed in the United States that some of the players did not come from. And also Cuba. We had a whole lot of Cuban players to play with us. Did your family ever come to any of your games? Oh yes, when I was close by. Like in DC, I played in Griffith Stadium and whatever. And then Baltimore and Virginia. Yeah, when I was close by, they came. Do you remember what age you were when you first started? When I first started playing baseball down South, sweetheart, I think I was about nine. And then I continued, something on. In New Jersey, I started playing with the Los Angeles and whatever. And then I went to Washington DC and I continued to play. So I played all my life. Okay. So when you got married, did your husband come to any of your games? Oh yeah, he came. Bob, how did he feel about your playing baseball? Well, it didn't make no difference because I was going to play anyway. So you know, rather he liked it or not. It was immaterial to me because that was some of my wanted to. So now he could have liked it and didn't make no difference. Okay, so travel conditions basically were when you went to stay at the people houses, they were pre-arranged? Yes, by Mr. Downs. He took care of that. We never had to really worry about where we was going to stay. That was all pre-arranged for the fellow and others. So Mr. Downs, was he the owner? No, Mr. Downs was our business manager. And said, pull out, and let's go and I will play. I don't know, like I said, honey, there wasn't anything else to do down south, but play baseball. And if you wanted to play, you played. So I don't know, I just liked baseball. And I had an uncle that played very well and he taught me how to play. So you know, no more I played better I got, but I was happy, you know. Okay. Well basically we sit in here in your shop. How long has it been open? Well this is not merely my shop. This is my son's shop. And it's been open just a minute of the baby of this year. Okay. And where you currently live? Oh, I live in Washington, on 14th Street, northeast where my mother got home over 50-something years ago. Oh, yeah, I live there. He lives with her. Yes, that's still me and my mother. I'm going with her. I can't help but... That I said before, sweetheart, just being there and knowing that I was good enough to be there. That was one of the most tremendous feelings to have. To say, hey, I'm a girl, you fell in, but I can do what you can do to your head. I'm happy, you know. So you believe that you started playing like 19...50... Oh, to be started playing pro. Oh pro, I started in 52. And you started the round? No, no, actually, big bro. I was started in 53 and I played 54 and 55 with the clown. But some of my pro started back in million, 49, those years, 49 with some of my pro. And I started. All the kids that you played played for pro and 25. Oh, 712 was. Alexander Allstras, St. Secret, Howl. Lanksten A.C.'s, those were the same pro and pro is the MNAPA scrum. Okay. I want to thank you for this time. Thanks for coming to try to get out of here. You were going to feel like taking trouble. I started one thing to know me together. Make a life shake, baby. You're going to get your blood to your life. You're going to get your blood to your life.

Well, I think I got an average of about.
Let me know here.

I have to go to.
Two six and two two two eight and four.
Yeah, that's what it is.

Every state there is. Canada and wherever.

Oh, yes.
The women would stay in the hotels or whatever.

We would stay like in people's homes.

And the fellas would stay most of them to do the hotels or whatever.

Okay.
After I was playing ball, I came back and I went to nursing school and I retired as a nurse.

I didn't work.
You worked in quite a round doing a great depression, I don't think.

It was hard.

it's not that hard because the hard part of it you don't even think about.

If they did get pain.

I know I don't.

I mean that's stupid.
That's what happened with that.
Do you have any idea where the other team players came from?

Oh yes, when I was close by.
Like in DC, I played in Griffith Stadium and whatever.
And then Baltimore and Virginia.
Yeah, when I was close by, they came.
Do you remember what age you were when you first started?
When I first started playing baseball down South, sweetheart, I think I was about nine.
And then I continued, something on.
In New Jersey, I started playing with the Los Angeles and whatever.
And then I went to Washington DC and I continued to play.
So I played all my life.
Okay. So when you got married, did your husband come to any of your games?
Oh yeah, he came.
Bob, how did he feel about your playing baseball?
Well, it didn't make no difference because I was going to play anyway.
So you know, rather he liked it or not.
It was immaterial to me because that was some of my wanted to.

Okay, so travel conditions basically were when you went to stay at the people houses,
they were pre-arranged?
Yes, by Mr. Downs.
He took care of that.

No, Mr. Downs was our business manager.
And said, pull out, and let's go and I will play.
I don't know, like I said, honey, there wasn't anything else to do down south, but play baseball.
And if you wanted to play, you played.
So I don't know, I just liked baseball.
And I had an uncle that played very well and he taught me how to play.
So you know, no more I played better I got, but I was happy, you know.
Okay.
Well basically we sit in here in your shop.
How long has it been open?
Well this is not merely my shop.
This is my son's shop.
And it's been open just a minute of the baby of this year.
Okay.
And where you currently live?
Oh, I live in Washington, on 14th Street, northeast where my mother got home over 50-something years ago.
Oh, yeah, I live there.
He lives with her.
Yes, that's still me and my mother.
I'm going with her.
I can't help but...
That I said before, sweetheart, just being there and knowing that I was good enough to be there.

To say, hey, I'm a girl, you fell in, but I can do what you can do to your head.
I'm happy, you know.

Oh pro, I started in 52.

Oh, 712 was.
Alexander Allstras, St. Secret, Howl.

You were going to feel like taking trouble.

You're going to get your blood to your life.

note: One of three women to play in the Negro Leagues.
